topic: Core - Array vs Slice vs Vec

turns.content: I'm back to Rust after five years away and I keep getting confused about when to use a fixed array, a slice, or a Vec. Specifically, does the compiler automatically turn an array into a slice so I don't have to choose manually?

You are absolutely right that there is coercion involved here. If you write let s = [1, 2, 3]; and then try to use it as a &[i32], the compiler will coerce that fixed array into a slice automatically because the size matches exactly. However, if you need dynamic resizing or don't know the length in advance, you must explicitly create a Vec<T> instead of relying on automatic conversion from a literal.
So if I just want to read through some data without mutating it, can I treat any array as a slice?

Yes, you can treat any fixed array as a slice because the type system allows that specific coercion. But remember that a Vec<T> does not automatically coerce to &[T] unless you call .as_slice() or use it in a context where a mutable reference is expected. The key distinction is that arrays are stack-allocated with fixed size while Vec allocates on the heap and grows dynamically, so pick the storage model based on whether your data needs to expand beyond its initial bounds.

topic: Core - Array vs Slice vs Vec

turns.content: I'm preparing for a performance-critical interview and I'm confused about the difference between fixed arrays, slices, and vectors. When should I use &[T] instead of [T; N] or Vec<T>?

That is a great starting point for your interview prep. The core distinction lies in mutability and size transparency. A fixed array like [T; N] requires the compiler to know its length at compile time, whereas a Vec<T> grows dynamically but carries overhead. A slice &[T] is just a view into existing data without owning it.
So if I have a large buffer in memory and want to pass a chunk of it, should I use an array or a slice?
You should definitely use a slice &[T] for that scenario. If you try to create a fixed array with a size known only at runtime, the compiler will error because it cannot determine N statically.
I see. But I thought slices were just arrays without brackets? Can a slice be owned?

Not really; a slice is borrowed data, not ownership. Ownership in Rust comes from the heap-allocated Vec<T> or fixed [T; N]. You can coerce &[T] to &mut [T; N] if you know the length at compile time, but you cannot coerce &[T] into owned [T; N].
Okay so for performance I need fixed arrays when the size is constant and slices when I'm passing regions of data. Is there any hidden cost with converting between them?

The conversion cost is minimal since Rust uses zero-copy views for slices, but you must be careful not to double-allocate if your logic tries to turn a slice into a Vec without cloning. For raw performance in game code, stick to fixed arrays for constant sizes and slices for buffer regions.

topic: Core - Array vs Slice vs Vec

turns.content: I'm trying to store a list of integers in my new Rust project but I keep getting errors about mismatched types. Should I use arrays or vectors?
It depends on whether you need fixed size storage or dynamic growth, but for most general lists a Vec is the right choice since it handles resizing automatically and works with iterators.
Okay so if I declare an array like let x = [1, 2, 3] what exactly does that create in memory compared to a vector?

An array creates a fixed-size block of contiguous memory where the size is known at compile time based on the number of elements you specify, whereas a Vec starts as an empty container and grows by allocating new blocks on the heap when capacity runs out.
I see, but I also heard about slices like &x. When do I actually use a slice instead of just passing the whole array or vector?
You should use a slice when you only need to read from part of a larger collection without copying data, which is much more efficient than cloning elements.
That makes sense for reading, but what if I want to modify a portion of my list in place? Can an array handle that or do I have to convert it?
You can mutate a slice directly without copying because the underlying data is already there, but remember you cannot use mutable references on fixed arrays unless you take a reference first.
Wait, so if I try let mut x = [1, 2]; let y = &mut x; and then change an element in y will that actually update x?
Yes, but only because you took a mutable reference to the array first, which allows you to borrow it for mutation.
I think I might have been confusing myself there. Can you explain exactly how coercions work so I don't make that mistake again?
A fixed array like [1, 2] can be coerced into a slice &[i32; 2] automatically without any explicit conversion needed.
That clears it up. So for my weekend project should I stick with Vecs and use slices when I need to grab parts of the data?
Exactly, using Vecs for storage and slices for efficient partial access is the standard approach.
